lines.greek: νῦν δέ μιν ἀκλειῶς ἅρπυιαι ἀνηρείψαντο·
οἴχετʼ ἄιστος ἄπυστος, ἐμοὶ δʼ ὀδύνας τε γόους τε
κάλλιπεν. οὐδέ τι κεῖνον ὀδυρόμενος στεναχίζω
οἶον, ἐπεί νύ μοι ἄλλα θεοὶ κακὰ κήδεʼ ἔτευξαν.
ὅσσοι γὰρ νήσοισιν ἐπικρατέουσιν ἄριστοι,
Δουλιχίῳ τε Σάμῃ τε καὶ ὑλήεντι Ζακύνθῳ,
ἠδʼ ὅσσοι κραναὴν Ἰθάκην κάτα κοιρανέουσιν,
τόσσοι μητέρʼ ἐμὴν μνῶνται, τρύχουσι δὲ οἶκον.
ἡ δʼ οὔτʼ ἀρνεῖται στυγερὸν γάμον οὔτε τελευτὴν
ποιῆσαι δύναται· τοὶ δὲ φθινύθουσιν ἔδοντες
οἶκον ἐμόν· τάχα δή με διαρραίσουσι καὶ αὐτόν.
τὸν δʼ ἐπαλαστήσασα προσηύδα Παλλὰς Ἀθήνη·
ὢ πόποι, ἦ δὴ πολλὸν ἀποιχομένου Ὀδυσῆος
δεύῃ, ὅ κε μνηστῆρσιν ἀναιδέσι χεῖρας ἐφείη.
εἰ γὰρ νῦν ἐλθὼν δόμου ἐν πρώτῃσι θύρῃσι
σταίη, ἔχων πήληκα καὶ ἀσπίδα καὶ δύο δοῦρε,
τοῖος ἐὼν οἷόν μιν ἐγὼ τὰ πρῶτʼ ἐνόησα
οἴκῳ ἐν ἡμετέρῳ πίνοντά τε τερπόμενόν τε,
ἐξ Ἐφύρης ἀνιόντα παρʼ Ἴλου Μερμερίδαο—
ᾤχετο γὰρ καὶ κεῖσε θοῆς ἐπὶ νηὸς Ὀδυσσεὺς

interlinear: but-now him gloriously-not the-Harpies have-snatched-away;
he-is-gone unseen, unheard-of, and-to-me pains and groans
he-left. nor for-that-man lamenting do-I-groan
alone, since now for-me other evil sorrows the-gods have-wrought.
for as-many-as over-the-islands hold-sway, the-best,
over-Dulichium and Same and wooded Zacynthus,
and-as-many-as rugged Ithaca throughout rule,
so-many my-mother woo, and-wear-away the-house.
and-she neither refuses the-hateful marriage nor an-end
to-make is-able; and-they waste-away, devouring
my house; and-soon indeed me they-will-destroy, myself also.'
and-him, greatly-troubled, addressed Pallas Athena:
'Ah me, surely indeed greatly of-departed Odysseus
you-have-need, who upon-the-wooers shameless his-hands might-lay.
for-if now coming, in the-house's outermost doors
he-should-stand, having a-helmet and a-shield and two spears,
such being as-him I first knew
in-house our, drinking and taking-delight,
from Ephyra returning, from Ilus son-of-Mermerus
(for he-had-gone also there on a-swift ship, Odysseus,
literal: But now the Harpies have snatched him away without glory;
he is gone unseen, unheard of, and he left me pains
and groans. Nor is it for him alone that I lament
and grieve, since the gods have wrought me other evil sorrows.
For all the noblest who hold sway over the islands,
over Dulichium and Same and wooded Zacynthus,
and all who rule throughout rugged Ithaca,
all these woo my mother and wear away the house.
And she neither refuses the hateful marriage, nor is able
to make an end of it; and they waste my house,
devouring it; and soon they will destroy me as well.'
Then Pallas Athena, greatly troubled, spoke to him:
'Ah me, surely you have great need of Odysseus, who is gone,
that he might lay his hands upon these shameless wooers.
For if he were now to come and stand at the outer doors
of the house, with a helmet and a shield and two spears,
such as he was when I first knew him
in our house, drinking and taking his delight,
returning from Ephyra, from Ilus son of Mermerus
(for Odysseus had gone there also in his swift ship,
translations.elizabethan:
But now the Harpies have caught him away
without a name, and he is gone from sight
and hearing, and has left me tears and pain.
Nor is it he alone I mourn. The gods
have shaped me other sorrows. All the lords
who hold the islands, Dulichium, Same,
and wooded Zacynthus, and all who rule
in rugged Ithaca, all these are here
to court my mother, and they waste the house.
She neither can refuse the hateful match
nor make an end of it; and they consume
my living, and will shortly ruin me."
Then Pallas Athena, in anger, spoke:
"Now by the gods, thou hast great need of him,
Odysseus gone so long, to lay his hands
upon these shameless suitors. Let him come
and stand but once within the outer door
with helm and shield and his two spears in hand,
the man I knew when first I looked on him
drinking and merry in our house at home,
new come from Ephyra, from Ilus' hall,
the son of Mermerus.
translations.modernist:
Now the Harpies have taken him and left him no name. He is gone. Nobody sees him. Nobody hears of him. He left me pain and crying.

And it is not only him. The gods made me other troubles. Every lord who runs the islands, Dulichium and Same and wooded Zacynthus, and every man who rules in rugged Ithaca, all of them are courting my mother and eating the house down. She will not say no to the marriage and she cannot end it. They are finishing off my property. Soon they will finish me too."

Pallas Athena was angry when she answered.

"You need him. You need Odysseus badly, to put his hands on these shameless men. Let him come and stand in the outer doorway one time, with a helmet and a shield and two spears. The way he was when I first saw him, drinking in our house and enjoying himself, on his way back from Ephyra, from the house of Ilus son of Mermerus."
translations.storybook:
But now the Harpies have snatched him away and left him without even a name. He is gone. Nobody sees him. Nobody hears of him. All he left me was pain and weeping.

And it is not only him, either. The gods gave me other troubles as well. Every single lord who rules the islands, Dulichium and Same and woody Zacynthus, and every man who rules in rocky Ithaca, all of them are here courting my mother, and eating our house right down to the floorboards. She will not say no to marrying, and she cannot make it stop. They are gobbling up everything we own, and quite soon they will gobble me up too."

Pallas Athena was very angry when she answered him.

"Oh, how you need him! How you need Odysseus back, to lay his hands on these shameless men. If only he would come and stand just once in the doorway, with his helmet on and his shield up and two spears in his hand, looking the way he did when I first saw him, drinking and laughing in our house, on his way home from Ephyra, from the house of Ilus, the son of Mermerus."

murray.text: For all the princes who hold sway over the islands—Dulichium and Same and wooded Zacynthus—and those who lord it over rocky Ithaca, all these woo my mother and lay waste my house. And she neither refuses the hateful marriage,

nor is she able to make an end; but they with feasting consume my substance: ere long they will bring me, too, to ruin.” Then, stirred to anger, Pallas Athena spoke to him:“Out on it! Thou hast of a truth sore need of Odysseus that is gone, that he might put forth his hands upon the shameless wooers.

Would that he might come now and take his stand at the outer gate of the house, with helmet and shield and two spears, such a man as he was when I first saw him in our house drinking and making merry, on his way back from Ephyre, from the house of Ilus, son of Mermerus.

For thither, too, went Odysseus in his swift ship in search of a deadly drug, that he might have wherewith to smear his bronze-tipped arrows; yet Ilus gave it not to him, for he stood in awe of the gods that are forever; but my father gave it, for he held him strangely dear.
